name: Jñānaśrīmitra
dates_notes: Conventionally dated c. 980–1040 CE on the basis of his position at Vikramaśīla under Atīśa's predecessors and on the citation chain (he is teacher of Ratnakīrti, who is dated c. 1000–1050). Frauwallner (1933), Thakur's (1959, 1987) editions of the *Jñānaśrīmitra-Nibandhāvalī*, and McCrea and Patil's (2010 Buddhist Philosophy of Language in India) treatment converge on this window.

lineage_polemical.note: Jñānaśrīmitra's *Īśvaravāda* is one of the principal late Buddhist refutations of the Naiyāyika Īśvara-arguments; Udayana's *Nyāya-Kusumāñjali* is in turn the Nyāya reply to this line of Buddhist critique. The Buddhist-Nyāya theological-epistemological debate is most fully documented in this exchange.

core_thesis: Jñānaśrīmitra is the principal Buddhist pramāṇavādin of the eleventh century and the senior exponent of the Vikramaśīla Yogācāra-Pramāṇa synthesis that closes the Indian Buddhist scholastic tradition. His twelve treatises (collected in the *Jñānaśrīmitra-Nibandhāvalī*) extend Dharmakīrti's analysis across the full range of issues then under dispute between Buddhists and the rising Navya-Nyāya. The *Apoha-Prakaraṇa* is the canonical late-period articulation of the Buddhist *apoha* (exclusion) account of universals: a general term ('cow') does not refer to a real universal but designates a class via the exclusion of what the term is not (not-non-cow), and the apparent unity of the class is therefore a constructed conceptual unity grounded in the structural similarity of the particulars rather than in their participation in a real *jāti*. The *Sākārasiddhi-Śāstra* defends the *sākāra-vijñāna-vāda* (the doctrine that cognitions carry internal images of their objects) against the *nirākāra* alternative that the school's later phase has internalized as a serious option — Jñānaśrīmitra here aligning with the Dharmapāla branch of Yogācāra against the Sthiramati line. The *Kṣaṇabhaṅgādhyāya* defends the doctrine of universal momentariness through the inference from existence to momentariness (*sattvānumāna*): whatever exists must be capable of causal efficacy, and only the momentary can be causally efficacious, therefore whatever exists is momentary. The *Īśvaravāda* refutes the Naiyāyika arguments for the existence of God. The *Vyāpti-Carcā* refines the Buddhist analysis of pervasion (*vyāpti*) — the universal-relation that grounds valid inference — and is the principal source for late Buddhist inferential theory. Together the corpus is the last great original synthesis of Indian Buddhist philosophy before the disappearance of the school from the subcontinent.

engaged_works.title: Jnanasrimitra-Nibandhavali
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Twelve treatises securely attributed to Jñānaśrīmitra; the collected Sanskrit text edited by Thakur (1959, revised 1987) on the basis of a single surviving manuscript discovered in Tibet. Without the Thakur edition the corpus would be largely lost.

ascription_evidence.description: Thakur 1959/1987 critical edition; McCrea and Patil 2010 translation and study of the Apoha-Prakaraṇa.

engaged_works.summary: Twelve self-standing prose treatises covering the principal disputed questions of eleventh-century Buddhist-Hindu debate. The *Apoha-Prakaraṇa* (treatment of exclusion) is the canonical late-period articulation of the Buddhist apoha-theory of universals and is the principal target of the Navya-Nyāya counter-argumentation (Gangeśa *Tattvacintāmaṇi*, *Apohavāda* section). The *Sākārasiddhi-Śāstra* defends the *sākāra* position in epistemology. The *Sākārasaṃgrahasūtra* supplies a compendious statement of the sākāra view. The *Yoginirṇaya-Prakaraṇa* treats the Yogācāra account of the yogic cognition of universals. The *Bhedābheda-Parīkṣā* refutes the Bhedābheda position on universals-and-particulars. The *Anupalabdhi-Rahasya* refines the Buddhist theory of non-perception as a pramāṇa. The *Kṣaṇabhaṅgādhyāya* defends universal momentariness. The *Vyāpti-Carcā* refines the analysis of vyāpti. The *Kāryakāraṇabhāvasiddhi* defends the Buddhist analysis of causality. The *Īśvaravāda* refutes Naiyāyika theism. The *Advaitabinduprakaraṇa* and the *Vyāpakānupalabdhi-Vicāra* round out the collection. The corpus is the largest single witness to late Indian Buddhist scholastic philosophy.

reception_notes: Jñānaśrīmitra is the senior teacher at Vikramaśīla in the immediately pre-Atīśan generation and is the principal Indian source for the Tibetan reception of late Buddhist pramāṇa. His *Apoha-Prakaraṇa* is the canonical late-period statement of the apoha-theory and the principal target of the Navya-Nyāya reply (Gangeśa *Tattvacintāmaṇi*, *Apohavāda* section, replies to Jñānaśrīmitra and Ratnakīrti by name). The Sanskrit Vedāntic engagement is limited; the principal interlocutors in this generation are the Naiyāyikas (Udayana, Vācaspati Miśra) rather than the Vedāntins.
